MAT 16:13.notes: First explicit 'Son of Man' question in Matthew's Caesarea Philippi pericope. ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου evokes Daniel 7:13-14 LXX where 'one like a son of man' (ὡς υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου) approaches the Ancient of Days and receives universal eschatological dominion. The choice of Caesarea Philippi as backdrop (a Greco-Roman religious center with Pan-grotto and Augustus-temple) sharpens the contrast: Jesus asks WHO this divine-Son-of-Man-figure-from-Daniel is, in the very place where competing divine-figures are venerated.

MAT 16:16.notes: Peter's confession ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ τοῦ ζῶντος invokes Davidic-covenant Son-of-God language: 2 Sam 7:14 ('I will be his father, and he shall be my son' — Davidic dynastic-promise) and Ps 2:7 ('You are my Son, today I have begotten you' — royal-coronation Psalm). The 'living God' (θεὸς ζῶν) qualifier echoes Deut 5:26, Josh 3:10, 1 Sam 17:26,36, Hos 2:1 LXX, Ps 84:3 LXX (frequent OT formula contrasting YHWH with dead-idols). At Caesarea Philippi — center of pagan worship — this confession is theologically maximal: the true God is alive and Jesus is his Davidic-Son.

MAT 16:18.notes: πύλαι ᾅδου ('gates of Hades') is a Septuagintal formula: Job 38:17 LXX (πύλαι θανάτου, parallel πυλωροὶ ᾅδου), Ps 9:14 LXX = MT 9:13 (πυλῶν τοῦ θανάτου), Isa 38:10 LXX (ἐν πύλαις ᾅδου, Hezekiah's lament). The LXX consistently uses πύλαι θανάτου / πύλαι ᾅδου for the realm-of-the-dead and its power. Jesus's promise that these gates will not prevail against his ἐκκλησία (FIRST OCCURRENCE in Matthew, in the Gospels) asserts that even death's domain cannot withstand the messianic-community founded on Peter's confession. The κατισχύω verb (3× NT) is rare and emphatic — 'overpower, prevail-against.'

MAT 16:19.notes: δώσω σοι τὰς κλεῖδας ('I will give you the keys') alludes to Isaiah 22:22 LXX, where YHWH transfers the 'key of the house of David' from Shebna to Eliakim with the formula 'he shall open and none shall shut, he shall shut and none shall open' (δώσω τὴν κλεῖδα οἴκου Δαυὶδ ἐπὶ τὸν ὦμον αὐτοῦ καὶ ἀνοίξει καὶ οὐκ ἔσται ὁ ἀποκλείων καὶ κλείσει καὶ οὐκ ἔσται ὁ ἀνοίγων). Cf. Rev 3:7 where the resurrected-Christ explicitly takes 'the key of David' to himself. The bind/loose terminology (δέω/λύω) is technical-rabbinic-halakhic for declaring forbidden/permitted; the periphrastic future-perfect (ἔσται δεδεμένον / λελυμένον) signals heaven's prior-judgment that earth's authority recognizes.

MAT 16:21.notes: First Matthean passion-prediction. Composite allusions: (1) Isaiah 53 Suffering Servant typology — the 'man of sorrows' rejected by elders/priests/scribes, killed for many, yet vindicated; (2) Hosea 6:2 LXX 'after two days he will revive us, on the third day he will raise us up' — the third-day-resurrection pattern; (3) Jonah 1:17-2:10 — three days in the great-fish belly, parallel to Matt 12:40 where Jesus draws this typology explicitly. δεῖ ('it-is-necessary') marks divine-necessity (cf. Lukan-Matthean usage for prophesied-events). The Sanhedrin trio (πρεσβύτεροι, ἀρχιερεῖς, γραμματεῖς) names the Jewish supreme-council that will engineer the crucifixion (cf. Matt 26:57-66, 27:1-2).

MAT 16:27.notes: Composite eschatological-judgment allusions: (1) DAN 7:13-14 LXX — Son-of-Man-coming-with-clouds receiving dominion-and-glory; key-Matthean source for 'Son of Man coming in the glory of his Father' (cf. also Matt 24:30, 25:31, 26:64); (2) ZEC 14:5 LXX 'the Lord my God will come, and all the holy ones with him' — divine-coming-with-angelic-retinue; (3) PSA 61:13 LXX = MT 62:12 'you, O Lord, will repay each according to his deeds' (σὺ ἀποδώσεις ἑκάστῳ κατὰ τὰ ἔργα αὐτοῦ); PRO 24:12 LXX 'who will not requite each according to his deeds?' Both Psalm and Proverbs use ἀποδίδωμι κατὰ + ἔργα/πρᾶξις — the very phrase Matthew echoes in 'ἀποδώσει ἑκάστῳ κατὰ τὴν πρᾶξιν αὐτοῦ.' Cf. Rom 2:6, Rev 22:12 for NT echoes.

MAT 17:2.notes: Two OT echoes layer in Matthew's transfiguration: (a) Exod 34:29 LXX δεδόξασται ἡ ὄψις τοῦ προσώπου αὐτοῦ — Moses's face-glow after Sinai theophany. Matthew's 'ἔλαμψεν τὸ πρόσωπον αὐτοῦ ὡς ὁ ἥλιος' is Matthean-unique over Mark and deliberately intensifies the Moses-parallel. (b) Dan 7:9 LXX τὸ ἔνδυμα αὐτοῦ ὡσεὶ χιὼν λευκόν — white-as-snow garment of the Ancient of Days. Matthew's 'ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ ἐγένετο λευκὰ ὡς τὸ φῶς' invokes the same apocalyptic-enthronement-glory imagery. Matthew's Jesus is BOTH new-Moses (face-glory) AND Ancient-of-Days figure (white-clothing-of-divine-glory) — double typology.

MAT 17:3.notes: Moses (Exod 24:15-18) and Elijah (1 Kgs 19:8-13) each had mountain-top encounters with God on Sinai/Horeb — the same mountain-tradition. Their joint appearance at Jesus's mountain-transfiguration creates a mountain-theophany-inclusio. Secondary: Moses+Elijah both represent categories (Law+Prophets) that Jesus fulfills — Matthew 5:17 programmatic. No direct verbal citation; a sustained narrative-typological echo.

MAT 17:4.notes: Peter's 'ποιήσω τρεῖς σκηνάς' (I will build three shelters) invokes σκηνή — the tabernacle-construction vocabulary of Exod 25-40 (LXX σκηνή for 'tent of meeting') and the Feast of Booths (Sukkot, Lev 23:39-43, LXX ἑορτὴ τῶν σκηνῶν). Peter's impulse is Torah-shaped: where God's glory dwells, a tabernacle must be built. Matthew preserves this Jewish-religious-instinct; Jesus's non-response redirects from localized-dwelling to the greater reality of divine presence in Christ himself. No verbal citation, but a theological-vocabulary echo worth database recording.

MAT 17:5.notes: Four OT threads converge in the voice-from-cloud: (1) Ps 2:7 LXX υἱός μου εἶ σύ — 'you are my son' (royal-messianic adoption). (2) Isa 42:1 LXX ὁ ἀγαπητός μου ὃν ᾑρέτισα, προσεδέξατο αὐτὸν ἡ ψυχή μου — 'my beloved, in whom my soul takes pleasure' (servant-song). These two combine behind 'ὁ υἱός μου ὁ ἀγαπητός, ἐν ᾧ εὐδόκησα' — established at Matt 3:17 and Mark 1:11 baptism, repeated here. (3) Deut 18:15 LXX αὐτοῦ ἀκούσεσθε — 'you shall listen to him' — Matthew's + Mark's addition 'ἀκούετε αὐτοῦ' at transfiguration directly quotes this prophet-like-Moses passage, identifying Jesus as the Moses-foretold prophet. (4) Exod 40:34-35 LXX ἐπεσκίαζεν ἐπ' αὐτὴν ἡ νεφέλη — 'the cloud overshadowed it' (Shekinah glory on tabernacle). Jesus's transfiguration is a new-Sinai + new-tabernacle moment.

MAT 17:6.notes: Prophetic face-down-in-fear response to direct theophany: Ezek 1:28 'I saw the appearance... and when I saw it I fell on my face'; Dan 10:9 'I fell on my face in a deep sleep.' The disciples' ἔπεσαν ἐπὶ πρόσωπον αὐτῶν καὶ ἐφοβήθησαν σφόδρα is typical-OT-prophetic-theophanic-response. Matthean addition over Mark/Luke deliberately locates the disciples in the prophetic tradition — they have just experienced a direct divine speech-theophany and respond with appropriate prostrate-fear. No verbal citation, but formulaic-scriptural response-pattern.

MAT 17:10.notes: The disciples' scribal-tradition 'Elijah must come first' reflects the scribes' standard interpretation of Mal 3:23-24 LXX (MT 4:5-6): καὶ ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ ἀποστελῶ ὑμῖν Ἠλίαν τὸν Θεσβίτην πρὶν ἐλθεῖν ἡμέραν κυρίου τὴν μεγάλην καὶ ἐπιφανῆ — 'I will send you Elijah the Tishbite before the great and manifest day of the Lord comes.' Second-temple Jewish expectation read this as a pre-messianic Elijah-appearance. The disciples, having just seen Elijah on the mountain, seek clarification. Matthew's 'οἱ γραμματεῖς λέγουσιν' (the scribes say) attributes the teaching to contemporary Jewish instruction, ultimately rooted in Malachi.

MAT 17:11.notes: Jesus's 'Ἠλίας μὲν ἔρχεται καὶ ἀποκαταστήσει πάντα' echoes Mal 4:6 LXX (3:24 MT): ἀποκαταστήσει καρδίαν πατρὸς πρὸς υἱόν — 'he will restore the heart of the father to the son.' The verb ἀποκαθίστημι is the direct LXX verb-pickup. Matthew's 'πάντα' (all-things) generalizes the Malachian restoration from the specific heart-turning to the broader eschatological-restoration-of-all-things (τὴν ἀποκατάστασιν πάντων Acts 3:21). Jesus affirms the scribal reading in principle while reassigning the fulfillment to John the Baptist (v12-13).

MAT 17:17.notes: 'Ὦ γενεὰ ἄπιστος καὶ διεστραμμένη' nearly verbatim echoes Deut 32:5 LXX (Song of Moses): γενεὰ σκολιὰ καὶ διεστραμμένη — 'a crooked and perverse generation.' Deut 32:20 LXX adds γενεὰ ἐξεστραμμένη ἐστίν υἱοί οἷς οὐκ ἔστιν πίστις ἐν αὐτοῖς — 'a perverse generation, sons in whom there is no faith.' Matthew's addition of 'διεστραμμένη' over Mark 9:19 sharpens the Deut 32 echo: Jesus positions himself as new-Moses rebuking wilderness-unfaithful-generation. The ἄπιστος adjective parallels Deut 32:20's οὐκ ... πίστις. Matthean new-Moses / new-Exodus typology — this generation of Israel is being judged as the wilderness generation was, with Jesus taking Moses's place.

MAT 18:5.notes: Prov 19:17 LXX 'ὁ ἐλεῶν πτωχὸν δανίζει θεῷ' (one who shows mercy to the poor lends to God) — divine-identification-with-marginal. Jesus here claims that reception-of-little-ones-in-his-name = reception-of-him. Christological high-watermark: Jesus takes YHWH-position as the one-with-whom-the-marginal-are-identified. Pattern culminates in Matt 25:40 'whatever you did for one of the least of these my brothers, you did for me.'

MAT 18:10.notes: Royal-court idiom of 'seeing the face of the king' — Est 1:14 LXX 'οἱ βλέποντες τὸ πρόσωπον τοῦ βασιλέως' (seven princes of Persia with direct royal access); Tob 12:15 'I am Raphael, one of the seven holy angels who stand in the presence of God' (Second-Temple angelology). Jesus transfers this privileged-court-access imagery to guardian-angels of 'little ones,' establishing that the seemingly-insignificant have the highest-possible divine-court representation. Also background: Ps 91:11 angel-protection; Dan 10-12 nation-angels; 1 Enoch 40.

MAT 18:13.notes: Isa 62:5 LXX 'ὡς εὐφραίνεται νυμφίος ἐπὶ νύμφῃ, οὕτως εὐφρανθήσεται κύριος ἐπὶ σοί' (as the bridegroom rejoices over the bride, so shall your God rejoice over you) — divine-joy-over-restoration motif. Background only; Matthew does not cite or echo lexically, but the theological pattern of divine-exultant-joy-over-recovered-people parallels the shepherd-rejoicing-over-one-found-sheep.

MAT 18:16.notes: Direct citation of Deut 19:15b LXX 'ἐπὶ στόματος δύο μαρτύρων καὶ ἐπὶ στόματος τριῶν μαρτύρων στήσεται πᾶν ῥῆμα' — Matthew compresses LXX's double-mention to 'ἐπὶ στόματος δύο μαρτύρων ἢ τριῶν σταθῇ πᾶν ῥῆμα' while preserving key-lexemes στόματος/μαρτύρων/σταθῇ/πᾶν ῥῆμα verbatim. Deuteronomic witness-law (Deut 17:6, 19:15) applied to church-discipline. Also re-cited: 2 Cor 13:1, 1 Tim 5:19, Heb 10:28.

MAT 18:20.notes: Jewish-rabbinic Shekhinah-tradition: Pirkei Avot 3:2 (attrib. R. Hananiah ben Teradion, c.135 AD) 'when two sit together and study Torah, the Shekhinah rests between them'; 3:6 'ten who sit together to study — Shekhinah among them.' Though the compilation is post-NT, the tradition likely reflects earlier oral-tradition. Jesus displaces the Shekhinah-slot by naming HIMSELF as the divine-presence-among-two-or-three — Christological claim to occupy YHWH's presence-function with his community. Matthean inclusio-frame: Matt 1:23 'Emmanuel / God-with-us' + Matt 28:20 'I am with you always' bracket the gospel; 18:20 is the keystone.

MAT 18:22.notes: Gen 4:24 LXX Lamech-song 'ἑβδομηκοντάκις ἑπτά' (77-fold-vengeance: 'If Cain is avenged sevenfold, truly Lamech 77-fold') — canonical text of escalating-human-vengeance in primeval-history. Jesus deliberately cites the same numeric-phrase to INVERT it: exponential-vengeance becomes exponential-forgiveness. CHRISTOLOGICAL: Jesus reverses the curse-of-Cain-line by establishing a counter-line of unlimited-mercy. Number 77 chosen specifically (not 70x7=490) to match Lamech's LXX-phrase precisely — Matthew expects audience to hear the echo.

MAT 18:30.notes: Amos 2:6 LXX 'they sell the righteous for silver, the needy for a pair of sandals' + Amos 8:6 similar + 2 Kgs 4:1 widow's-family-seized-for-debt — OT-prophetic background condemning creditor-cruelty. Matthew does not lexically cite but the MORAL-LOGIC of prophetic-denunciation of debt-imprisonment underlies the parable's moral-force.

MAT 18:31.notes: Gen 4:10 LXX 'φωνὴ αἵματος τοῦ ἀδελφοῦ σου βοᾷ πρός με ἐκ τῆς γῆς' (the voice of your brother's blood cries out to me from the ground) — Abel's blood-crying-out paradigm + Exod 2:23 LXX Israel's-groaning-ascending-to-God. Background for the mercy-refused is mercy-revealed-to-the-higher-authority principle — injustice does not stay hidden but reaches the Sovereign. The σύνδουλοι's report to the king models the way-the-oppressed's-cry reaches God.

MAT 18:33.notes: Exod 34:6 LXX core-theophany 'κύριος ὁ θεὸς οἰκτίρμων καὶ ἐλεήμων, μακρόθυμος καὶ πολυέλεος καὶ ἀληθινός' (the Lord, merciful and compassionate, long-suffering and abundant-in-mercy and true) + Lev 11:44-45 imitatio-Dei principle 'be holy as I am holy.' Jesus (through the parable-king) applies mercy-reciprocity: those who-received-mercy must-extend-mercy (cf. Matt 5:7 Beatitude; Luke 6:36 'be merciful as your Father is merciful'). The parable dramatizes Matt 6:14-15's Lord's-Prayer-appendix: 'if you forgive men their trespasses, your Father will also forgive you; but if you do not forgive men their trespasses, neither will your Father forgive your trespasses.'

MAT 19:4.notes: DIRECT citation of Gen 1:27 LXX 'ἄρσεν καὶ θῆλυ ἐποίησεν αὐτούς' — Jesus's creational-basis for marriage-indissolubility. Matthew preserves LXX wording verbatim. Jesus's rhetorical-strategy bypasses Deut-24 (Pharisees' proof-text) to ground marriage in Gen-1-2 creation-order. Paired with 19:5 GEN 2:24 as double-witness (cf. Deut 19:15 two-witnesses-standard).

MAT 19:5.notes: DIRECT citation of Gen 2:24 LXX 'ἕνεκεν τούτου καταλείψει ἄνθρωπος τὸν πατέρα αὐτοῦ καὶ τὴν μητέρα καὶ προσκολληθήσεται πρὸς τὴν γυναῖκα αὐτοῦ, καὶ ἔσονται οἱ δύο εἰς σάρκα μίαν.' Matthew has κολληθήσεται (simple) where LXX has προσκολληθήσεται (compound); otherwise verbatim. Jesus re-anchors Gen 2:24's 'for-this-reason' backward-referent to Gen 1:27 (v.4 citation) unifying the argument. The 'one flesh' metaphor covers bodily, emotional, social, and covenantal union — not narrowly-sexual. Also cited at 1 Cor 6:16, Eph 5:31.

MAT 19:18.notes: DIRECT citation of Decalogue commandments 6-9 verbatim-LXX in Matthean-Exodus-order (murder, adultery, theft, false-witness). Exod 20:13-16 LXX = Deut 5:17-20 LXX (identical wording). Matthew presents the four second-tablet-neighbor-prohibitions. Mark 10:19 adds 'Do-not-defraud' (μὴ ἀποστερήσῃς) — not-from-Decalogue but from Deut 24:14 or Lev 19:13 re wages-of-hired-servant; Matthew omits this. Luke 18:20 follows Matthew-order.

MAT 19:19.notes: DIRECT composite citation: (1) Exod 20:12 LXX / Deut 5:16 LXX 'Τίμα τὸν πατέρα καὶ τὴν μητέρα' — 5th commandment; Matthew drops the 'σου' (your) possessive. (2) Lev 19:18 LXX 'ἀγαπήσεις τὸν πλησίον σου ὡς σεαυτόν' — the love-neighbor commandment Jesus pairs with the Shema as the greatest-commandments at Matt 22:37-40. MATTHEAN-ONLY ADDITION: Mark 10:19 / Luke 18:20 list only the neighbor-harm-prohibitions (no 5th commandment, no Lev-19:18). Matthew UNIQUELY ADDS the love-your-neighbor here, pre-echoing 22:37-40 and setting up the v.21 sell-all-and-give-to-poor test as a direct-neighbor-love check. Lev 19:18 is also cited at Matt 5:43, 22:39, Mark 12:31, Luke 10:27, Rom 13:9, Gal 5:14, Jas 2:8.

MAT 19:26.notes: Jesus's statement 'παρὰ θεῷ πάντα δυνατά' (with-God-all-things-possible) wed (1) Gen 18:14 LXX 'μὴ ἀδυνατεῖ παρὰ τῷ θεῷ ῥῆμα' (Is-anything-impossible-with-God? — said to Sarah about Isaac's conception) and (2) Job 42:2 LXX 'οἶδα ὅτι πάντα δύνασαι' (Job's confession 'I know that you can do all things'). Matthew's wording combines both: παρὰ-θεῷ-πάντα-δυνατά weds Gen 18's παρὰ-θεῷ with Job 42's πάντα-δυνατά. SOTERIOLOGICAL-PIVOT: Jesus declares salvation a divine-work not a human-achievement — a foundational grace-theology text.

MAT 20:15.notes: SEMITIC-IDIOM "evil eye" (ὀφθαλμὸς πονηρός) for envy/stinginess grounded in: (1) Deut 15:9 LXX πονηρεύσηται ὁ ὀφθαλμός σου ("your eye becomes evil") — the sabbatical-year Mosaic warning not to begrudge the poor when the release-year approaches; (2) Prov 23:6 LXX μὴ συνδείπνει ἀνδρὶ βασκάνῳ (with-a-begrudging-eye-man do-not-eat); (3) Prov 28:22 LXX σπεύδει πλουτεῖν ἀνὴρ βάσκανος (a-man-with-an-evil-eye-hastens-to-be-rich). The idiom contrasts ὀφθαλμὸς ἁπλοῦς / ἀγαθός (Matt 6:22 "single/good eye" = generous heart) with πονηρός (envious/stingy heart). Jesus's parable-master rebukes the grumbling worker for envy-of-another's-gift — same vocabulary Jesus uses at Matt 6:22-23 (light-of-body-is-eye). Thematic-allusion, not citation.

MAT 20:18.notes: DIVINE-PASSIVE παραδοθήσεται ("will-be-handed-over") echoes Isa 53:6 LXX "κύριος παρέδωκεν αὐτὸν ταῖς ἁμαρτίαις ἡμῶν" and Isa 53:12 LXX "παρεδόθη εἰς θάνατον ἡ ψυχὴ αὐτοῦ" — the Suffering-Servant hand-over pattern. Matthew's third passion-prediction frames Jesus's betrayal as Servant-Song fulfillment. The same verb παραδίδωμι recurs v.19 παραδώσουσιν (active — chief-priests hand-to-Gentiles), completing the double-handoff.

MAT 20:19.notes: Jesus's prediction of Gentile-crucifixion "numbered with transgressors" (Isa 53:12 LXX "ἐν τοῖς ἀνόμοις ἐλογίσθη") anticipates Roman-execution under Gentile-magistrate. SBLGNT σταυρόω first-time on Jesus's lips here (expanding on 16:21/17:22-23 which used generic ἀποκτείνω "kill"). The mocking-scourging-crucifying triplet fulfilled in Matt 27:26-38. Thematic-background, not direct-citation — but frames the passion within the Servant-Song pattern.

MAT 20:21.notes: The mother's request for her sons to sit "at your right hand and at your left hand in your kingdom" invokes Ps 110:1 LXX "κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου" (sit-at-my-right) — the Messianic-enthronement-oracle Jesus himself applies to υἱὸς Δαυίδ at Matt 22:44. The mother's THEOLOGY is correct (Jesus WILL be enthroned as Davidic-Messiah per Ps 110) but her TIMING and REQUEST are mistaken (Jesus does not assign honor-positions; Father prepares them per v.23). Matthew's Jesus confirms his enthronement at 25:31 ("καθίσει ἐπὶ θρόνου δόξης αὐτοῦ") and 26:64 ("ἐκ δεξιῶν καθήμενον τῆς δυνάμεως"). Thematic-allusion.

MAT 20:22.notes: The "cup" (ποτήριον) Jesus is about-to-drink activates multiple OT cup-of-wrath texts: (1) Ps 75:8 LXX "ποτήριον ἐν χειρὶ κυρίου οἴνου ἀκράτου πλῆρες κεράσματος" (cup-in-YHWH's-hand full-of-unmixed-wine); (2) Isa 51:17, 22 LXX "τὸ ποτήριον τοῦ θυμοῦ" (the-cup-of-wrath) — Jerusalem drinks then wrath transferred to her persecutors; (3) Jer 25:15-28 LXX cup-of-wrath served to all-nations; (4) Ezk 23:31-34 cup-of-Samaria-Jerusalem judgment. Jesus drinks the cup his people deserved — substitutionary-judgment imagery re-confirmed at Gethsemane (Matt 26:39, 42 "πάτερ μου, εἰ δυνατόν, παρελθάτω ἀπ' ἐμοῦ τὸ ποτήριον τοῦτο"). Thematic-allusion — not a specific-verbatim-quote but a RICH OT-figure-cluster Matthew expects readers to hear.

MAT 20:28.notes:
FOUNDATIONAL CHRISTOLOGICAL SAYING. Three layers of OT background:
(1) Isa 53:10-12 LXX Servant-Song: "τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ" (his-soul/life, cf. Matt τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ); "παρεδόθη εἰς θάνατον"; "ἁμαρτίας πολλῶν ἀνήνεγκεν" (many-many ΠΟΛΛΩΝ-ΠΟΛΛΟΙΣ echoing ἀντὶ πολλῶν here); "ἐδικαίωσεν δίκαιον εὖ δουλεύοντα πολλοῖς". Matthew's λύτρον + ψυχή + πολλῶν = deliberate Servant-Song compression.
(2) Exo 21:30 LXX — λύτρα for a bull-goring-victim (ransom-price paid by owner).
(3) Num 18:15-16 LXX — λύτρον for firstborn-redemption (5 shekels).
(4) Ps 49:8 LXX "τίς δώσει ἐξίλασμα τῇ ψυχῇ αὐτοῦ" (who-will-give-ransom-for-his-soul) — rhetorical-impossibility: a human cannot pay-ransom for his brother.
λύτρον only 2x NT (here + Mark 10:45 verbatim). ἀντί = SUBSTITUTIONARY-preposition (in-place-of, not just ὑπέρ on-behalf-of) — cornerstone of Protestant penal-substitutionary-atonement doctrine. "Many" (πολλῶν) is the Servant-Song corporate-beneficiary marker, not a limitation (cf. ὁ πολύς Semitic inclusive-idiom).

MAT 20:30.notes: Υἱὸς Δαυίδ = Davidic-Messiah vocative rooted in: (1) 2 Sam 7:12-13 LXX Davidic-covenant promise; (2) Isa 11:1-10 LXX shoot-from-stump-of-Jesse oracle; (3) Jer 23:5-6 LXX righteous-branch-of-David; (4) Ps 89:3-4 LXX covenant-with-David-established-forever. The two blind-men publicly confess Jesus as Davidic-Messiah — the same confession made earlier by Canaanite-woman (Matt 15:22) but here in RUN-UP to Jerusalem triumphal-entry (21:9 "Ὡσαννὰ τῷ υἱῷ Δαυίδ"). Irony: the blind SEE Messianic-identity; the sighted crowd rebukes them (v.31). Repeated at v.31. Thematic-allusion.

MAT 21:5.notes: Opens with verbatim Isa 62:11 LXX εἴπατε τῇ θυγατρὶ Σιών, continues with Zech 9:9 LXX for king-on-donkey. Matthew drops LXX's "Rejoice greatly, daughter of Zion; shout, daughter of Jerusalem" and "righteous and saving" — compressed for narrative-flow. πραΰς preserved from LXX (MT עָנִי afflicted/humble); ἐπιβεβηκώς retained. The poetic-parallelism of one-animal (donkey + colt = poetic synonyms in Hebrew) Matthew reads as TWO actual animals (v.2, 7), distinctive-Matthean-exegesis. Composite-citation confirms Matthean-method of blending-prophetic-sources.

MAT 21:8.notes: 2 Kings 9:13 LXX: Israelites spread their garments (ἱμάτια) under Jehu as he was anointed king-of-Israel by Elisha's messenger. Matthew's crowd-gesture with spread-cloaks for Jesus echoes this royal-anointing-scene. Not a verbal-citation but cultural-gesture-parallel: royal acclamation via garment-spreading.

MAT 21:9.greek_matches: LXX (verbatim εὐλογημένος ὁ ἐρχόμενος ἐν ὀνόματι κυρίου)
MAT 21:9.notes: Crowd's Hallel-acclamation as Jesus enters Jerusalem. Ps 118 is the final Hallel-psalm sung at Passover (Pss 113-118). Originally pilgrim-welcome-formula; crowds apply to Jesus as ὁ ἐρχόμενος (the coming-one = Messianic title, cf. Matt 11:3). Combined with Hosanna (transliteration of Heb הוֹשִׁיעָה־נָּא "save now") and υἱῷ Δαυίδ Messianic-title, crowd affirms Jesus as Davidic-Messiah. Quote-block inside crowd-speech.

MAT 21:13.greek_matches: LXX (Isa verbatim minus πᾶσιν τοῖς ἔθνεσιν; Jer as allusive-embedding)
MAT 21:13.notes: Isa 56:7 LXX ὁ οἶκός μου οἶκος προσευχῆς κληθήσεται — Matthew drops LXX πᾶσιν τοῖς ἔθνεσιν (for all nations); Mark 11:17 retains it. Isa 56 is universal-invitation passage — all peoples welcomed to temple-prayer; Matthew's omission doesn't cancel but compresses. Second half adapts Jer 7:11 LXX μὴ σπήλαιον λῃστῶν ὁ οἶκός μου — Jer 7 is famous Temple-Sermon denouncing temple-as-false-refuge. Jesus's composite-citation implicitly threatens temple-destruction (Jer 7 → exile) while calling back to temple-purpose (Isa 56 → universal prayer). Christological: Jesus speaks as YHWH ("my house"). Quote-nesting: outer « » Jesus-speech, inner ‹ › Isa 56 citation.

MAT 21:16.greek_matches: LXX (= Ps 8:3 MT; Matthew follows LXX κατηρτίσω αἶνον against MT יִסַּדְתָּ־עֹז established-strength)
MAT 21:16.notes: CHRISTOLOGICAL HIGH-WATERMARK. Jesus applies to himself a Psalm-verse originally addressed to YHWH by the Psalmist. Ps 8 celebrates Creator-God whose-praise is proclaimed by infants; Jesus legitimizes children's Hosanna-to-Son-of-David by citing this psalm — IMPLYING that the praise rightly belongs to him AS GOD. Matthew-specific-divergence from MT: Matthew's αἶνος (praise) follows LXX; MT has עֹז (strength-citadel). Per NT usage, LXX-form fits Christian-application of praise-legitimation better. Quote-block: « » outer Jesus-reply, inner ‹ › Ps 8 citation.

MAT 21:32.notes: Prov 8:20 LXX ἐν ὁδοῖς δικαιοσύνης περιπατῶ (Wisdom walks in ways-of-righteousness); Prov 16:31 LXX ὁδὸς δικαιοσύνης (way-of-righteousness). Matthew's ἐν ὁδῷ δικαιοσύνης describes John's prophetic-calling — OT Wisdom-Literature idiom for righteous-life. Not direct-citation but idiomatic-echo from Proverbs.

MAT 21:33.greek_matches: LXX (verbatim vineyard-construction-vocabulary: ἐφύτευσεν ἀμπελῶνα, φραγμόν, ληνόν, πύργον)
MAT 21:33.notes: Parable of Wicked Tenants opens with direct-verbal-echoes of Isaiah's Vineyard-Song (Isa 5:1-7). Every Greek noun matches LXX-Isaiah exactly: φυτεύω + ἀμπελών, φραγμός περιτίθημι, ληνὸν ὀρύσσω, πύργον οἰκοδομέω. Hearers would recognize Isaiah's framework immediately — Isaiah 5 indicts Israel for bad-grapes-from-good-vineyard; Jesus's parable redirects the indictment from the vineyard itself (Isa) to its TENANTS (priests/leaders). Matthean redactional-shift of Isaianic-judgment-tradition. Sets up v.43 pronouncement that kingdom will be transferred ἔθνει.

MAT 21:34.notes: Isa 5:2 LXX ἤλπιζεν ἵνα ποιήσῃ σταφυλήν (expected-to-produce grapes). Matthew's owner-sending-slaves to collect fruit (καρποὶ ἀπό) echoes Isaianic vineyard-harvest-expectation. Linked with MAT 21:33 (main Isa 5:1-2 allusion) but v.34-specific expectation-to-receive-fruit. Not separate citation — extension of 21:33 allusion-frame.

MAT 21:42.notes: CHRISTOLOGICAL HIGH-WATERMARK. Jesus identifies himself as the rejected-stone-become-cornerstone. SAME Psalm 118 whose v.26 the crowds quoted at v.9 — closed-Christological-loop for the chapter: (a) entering Messiah is blessed-one (Ps 118:26, v.9), (b) rejected-stone becomes cornerstone (Ps 118:22-23, v.42). Ps 118 is HALLEL (Passover psalm). Original referent likely Israel-as-nation-rejected-by-superpowers-but-vindicated-by-YHWH; Jesus reapplies individually: himself as rejected-by-builders (= chief priests), vindicated-by-God. NT re-use widespread: Acts 4:11, 1 Pet 2:7, Rom 9:33 (partial). Stone-metaphor preserved per uW figs-metaphor. Christological center: death-and-resurrection are Jesus's rejection-and-vindication.

MAT 21:44.notes: Double-image stone-judgment: (1) ISA 8:14-15 LXX stone-of-stumbling λίθου προσκόμματι over which Israel-will-fall-and-be-shattered (συντριβήσονται); Matthew's συνθλασθήσεται cognate-violence. (2) DAN 2:34-35, 44-45 stone cut-from-mountain-without-hands that STRIKES the-statue (representing earthly-kingdoms) and grinds-it-to-chaff (λικμήσει echoes Daniel's grinding-chaff-motif). Combined: Jesus is both stumbling-stone (those who reject him fall-and-shatter, Isa) and judging-stone (at eschaton falls-on-nations-and-crushes, Dan). TEXTUAL NOTE: v.44 is INCLUSION-VARIANT — SBLGNT/NA28/BSB INCLUDE (supported by ℵ, B, C, L, W, Z, majority MSS); Western-text D + OL + Syriac OMIT (probably accidental homoioteleuton). Per uW and mainstream-critical-consensus, include without brackets.

MAT 22:7.notes: King-sending-armies-to-destroy-rebels-and-burn-city echoes OT covenant-curse-typology (LEV 26, DEU 28) and specific historical-judgment-on-Jerusalem patterns (2KI 25:8-9 Nebuchadnezzar burns Jerusalem; ISA 10:6 Assyria-as-rod-of-judgment). Majority-evangelical reading: post-eventu prophetic-reference to Titus's destruction of Jerusalem AD 70 (Matthew writing c.80-85). Matthean addition (absent in Luke 14 parallel), sharpening the prophetic-application against first-century Israel.

MAT 22:21.notes: Jesus's εἰκών-argument implicitly leverages GEN 1:26-27 LXX κατ' εἰκόνα theological-anthropology. The coin bears Caesar's εἰκών, so render it to Caesar; but humans bear God's εἰκών, so render THEMSELVES to God. The theological-depth is not on the surface but embedded — Jesus plays on εἰκών-as-ownership-marker, a point scribal hearers would have caught. Second-temple Jewish use of GEN 1:26-27 in ethical-political argumentation attested in rabbinic sources (m.Sanh 4:5: each human bears divine-image).

MAT 22:24.greek_matches: LXX (DEU 25:5-6 levirate-law + GEN 38:8 Onan's σπέρμα-ἀνίστημι phrase)
MAT 22:24.notes: Sadducees' paraphrase-summary of Mosaic levirate-law. Primary source DEU 25:5-6 LXX establishes the institution (brother marries widow for sake of deceased-brother's-name-continuation); secondary echo GEN 38:8 LXX (Judah to Onan: ἀνάστησον σπέρμα τῷ ἀδελφῷ σου = raise up seed for your brother — verbatim phrase Sadducees use here). Sadducees cite Torah since they rejected authority of Prophets+Writings — strategic use of their-accepted-canon.

MAT 22:32.greek_matches: LXX verbatim (EXO 3:6 LXX: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ θεὸς τοῦ πατρός σου, θεὸς Αβρααμ καὶ θεὸς Ισαακ καὶ θεὸς Ιακωβ)
MAT 22:32.notes: KEY EXEGETICAL CRUX. Jesus's proof-of-resurrection from Pentateuch (Sadducees' accepted canon). Argument hinges on PRESENT-TENSE ἐγώ εἰμι spoken to Moses ~500 years after patriarchs died. If they ceased to exist at death, God could not be in ongoing covenantal relationship with them. Resurrection (not mere soul-immortality) required because covenant was with whole person. Rabbinic argument-from-verb-tense (qal vaḥomer-style inference) impressed crowds (v.33). Matthew's quotation uses singular θεός per ὁ θεὸς Ἀβραὰμ καὶ ὁ θεὸς Ἰσαὰκ καὶ ὁ θεὸς Ἰακώβ, triple-repetition-pattern standard in LXX EXO 3:6, 3:15, 3:16.

MAT 22:37.greek_matches: LXX (with Matthean-διάνοια variation from LXX δυνάμεως or διανοίας depending on MS)
MAT 22:37.notes: The Shema (DEU 6:4-9) foundational Jewish-daily-confession. LXX DEU 6:5 MS tradition: some MSS have καρδία/ψυχή/δύναμις (heart/soul/strength, matching MT לבב/נפש/מאד); other MSS have διανοία (mind). Matthew has καρδία/ψυχή/διάνοια three-term (Mark 12:30 has four-term including ἰσχύς; Luke 10:27 also four-term). Matthean three-fold may interpret the Hebrew מְאֹד (might/very-much) as intellectual-commitment — fitting that Jesus cites to a νομικός (legal-expert).

MAT 22:39.greek_matches: LXX verbatim (LEV 19:18 LXX: ἀγαπήσεις τὸν πλησίον σου ὡς σεαυτόν)
MAT 22:39.notes: Jesus's combination of Shema (DEU 6:5) with LEV 19:18 as summary-of-all-Torah is distinctive. Intertestamental-parallels (Testament of Issachar 5:2, Testament of Dan 5:3) may predate or reflect Christian-influence. Jesus's expansive reading of πλησίον (beyond ethnic-Israelite) is explicit in LUK 10:29-37 Good-Samaritan. This combination becomes foundational for Christian-ethics (Rom 13:9, Gal 5:14, Jas 2:8).

MAT 22:42.notes: Pharisees' answer 'David's son' reflects the Davidic-Messiah-tradition rooted in 2SA 7:12-16 (Davidic covenant promise), ISA 11:1 (root-of-Jesse), JER 23:5 (righteous-Branch for David), EZK 34:23-24 (Davidic-shepherd). Universally-accepted rabbinic-doctrine (cf. Psalms of Solomon 17-18, 4Q Florilegium). Jesus's counter in vv.43-45 will show this is true-but-incomplete — Messiah must be MORE than David's-descendant.

MAT 22:44.greek_matches: LXX verbatim (PSA 109:1 LXX: εἶπεν ὁ κύριος τῷ κυρίῳ μου Κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου ἕως ἂν θῶ τοὺς ἐχθρούς σου ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν σου — Matthew has ὑποκάτω for LXX ὑποπόδιον)
MAT 22:44.notes: Most-cited OT verse in NT (~30x direct+allusion). CHRISTOLOGICAL HIGH-WATERMARK: Jesus implies Messiah (= himself) is addressed by David as κύριος, therefore more-than-mere-descendant. MT לַאדֹנִי lower-tier lord-title distinct from יהוה Tetragrammaton — LXX collapses both to κύριος, and Jesus exploits this to argue Messiah is addressed in Lordship-terms. The ULT+UST convention uppercases the second κύριος for Messiah-referent. Jesus uses ὑποκάτω (under) where LXX has ὑποπόδιον (footstool) — minor rephrasing that preserves meaning. First major NT use of what becomes the PSA 110:1 enthronement-paradigm applied to Jesus at Acts 2:34-35, HEB 1:13, 1CO 15:25, etc.

MAT 23:5.notes: Pharisaic enlargement-of-tefillin and lengthening-of-tzitzit presupposes the Torah-commands: NUM 15:38-39 commands tassels (tzitzit) on the four corners of garments to remind Israelites of the LORD's commandments; DEU 6:8 commands binding the words 'as a sign on your hand and as frontlets between your eyes' (basis for tefillin/phylacteries); DEU 11:18 repeats the binding-as-sign command. Jesus's critique is not that Pharisees observed these commands but that they made them ostentatious-status-displays.

MAT 23:12.notes: Jesus's exalt-humble reversal-maxim draws on the broad OT wisdom-and-prophetic stream: EZK 21:26 'exalt the low and abase the high'; ISA 2:11,17 'haughty looks of man shall be brought low'; PRO 3:34 'God scorns the scornful but gives grace to the humble' (cited at JAS 4:6 and 1PE 5:5 LXX); JOB 22:29 'one is humbled, you say there is lifting up.' Pauline+Petrine NT-reception reinforces this as a foundational Christian-formation-principle.

MAT 23:22.notes: Jesus's argument that swearing-by-heaven invokes God's throne presupposes ISA 66:1 LXX (ὁ οὐρανός μοι θρόνος, ἡ δὲ γῆ ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν μου = 'heaven is my throne, the earth is my footstool'). Same OT background informs MAT 5:34-35 (parallel teaching: 'do not swear by heaven for it is God's throne, nor by earth for it is his footstool'). Matthean-internal cross-reference signals Jesus's consistent oath-theology rooted in Isaian theology of God's-cosmic-presence.

MAT 23:23.notes: Jesus's triad κρίσις/ἔλεος/πίστις (justice/mercy/faithfulness) echoes the Hebrew-prophetic triad mishpat/chesed/emunah found in MIC 6:8 (do justice, love mercy, walk humbly with God), HOS 6:6 (I desire mercy not sacrifice, knowledge of God rather than burnt offerings — Jesus already cited at MAT 9:13 and 12:7), and ZEC 7:9 (render true judgment, show kindness and mercy each to his brother). Jesus's prioritization of these over ritual-tithing recapitulates the prophetic-tradition's critique of cult-without-justice. The Matthean quotation πίστιν (faithfulness) corresponds best to Hebrew אֱמוּנָה (covenant-loyalty) rather than personal-belief.

MAT 23:35.greek_matches: no-verbatim — Abel-to-Zechariah merism spans the Hebrew canon (Genesis to 2-Chronicles)
MAT 23:35.notes: FIRST-AND-LAST righteous-blood. GEN 4:8-10 records Abel's murder by Cain — the first righteous victim, with God saying Abel's-blood cries-from-the-ground. 2CH 24:20-22 records Zechariah son of Jehoiada being stoned in the temple-court by King Joash for prophetic-rebuke (note name-discrepancy: 2-Chr says 'son of Jehoiada' but Matthew says 'son of Berechiah' — possibly conflation with the prophet Zechariah-son-of-Berechiah, ZEC 1:1, whose martyrdom is not biblically-recorded but is in some-Jewish-tradition; or 'Berechiah' is an alternate-name for Jehoiada; or scribal-error). The merism is structurally significant: in the Hebrew-Bible-order, Genesis is the FIRST book and 2-Chronicles is the LAST — so Abel-to-Zechariah covers the entire-Tanakh A-to-Z.

MAT 23:37.notes: Jesus's hen-and-chicks simile draws on the OT-bird-of-protection imagery cluster. DEU 32:11 LXX uses eagle-imagery (ἀετός) for God's protection of Israel (eagle-stirring-nest, hovering, spreading wings). PSA 91:4 LXX uses ὑπὸ τὰς πτέρυγας αὐτοῦ (under-his-wings) — same lexical pattern Jesus uses (ὑπὸ τὰς πτέρυγας). PSA 17:8 'hide me in the shadow of your wings'; PSA 36:7 'children of men take refuge in shadow of your wings'; PSA 57:1 'in shadow of your wings I take refuge'; RUT 2:12 'come to take refuge under his wings'. Jesus uses ὄρνις (hen) rather than eagle — gentler/more-domestic image, fitting the maternal-tender tone of the lament.

MAT 23:38.notes: Jesus's declaration that 'your house is left desolate' echoes the OT pattern of Shekinah-glory-departing-the-temple before judgment-and-destruction. EZK 10:18-19 + 11:22-23: the glory-of-the-LORD departs from above the cherubim, from the threshold, then from the city, ascending the Mount of Olives — exactly the geography of Jesus's discourse (delivered in Jerusalem, with Olivet-Discourse to follow in chs.24-25 from the Mount of Olives, MAT 24:3). JER 12:7 'I have forsaken my house, I have abandoned my heritage'; JER 22:5 'this house shall become a desolation' — direct OT vocabulary for divine-abandonment of the temple-house preceding destruction. Fulfilled at AD 70.

MAT 23:39.greek_matches: LXX verbatim (PSA 117:26 LXX: εὐλογημένος ὁ ἐρχόμενος ἐν ὀνόματι κυρίου)
MAT 23:39.notes: DIRECT CITATION of PSA 118:26 LXX (= PSA 117:26 in LXX numbering). Same exact words the crowds shouted at the Triumphal Entry just-days-prior (MAT 21:9 / MRK 11:9 / LUK 19:38 / JOH 12:13). PSA 118 is the climactic Hallel-psalm (PSA 113-118) sung at Passover. The Hallel-formula 'Blessed-is-he-who-comes-in-the-name-of-the-LORD' was the priestly-greeting to pilgrims arriving for festivals — Jesus turns it into eschatological-prophecy: a future-day when Israel will recognize and welcome him. Pauline parallel: ROM 11:25-27 (all-Israel-will-be-saved). Per the 'until-they-say' clause — two readings: (1) future-recognition-promised, (2) future-recognition-conditional-for-seeing. Most-modern-readers favor (1).

MAT 24:8.greek_matches: no-verbatim — stock prophetic birth-pain-metaphor for eschatological distress
MAT 24:8.notes: ὠδίνες (birth pains) as eschatological-distress metaphor draws on prophetic imagery: ISA 13:8 LXX uses ὠδίνες for the pangs that will seize the day-of-the-Lord against Babylon; ISA 26:17 LXX pictures Israel in-labor; JER 4:31 LXX describes Jerusalem's ὠδίνοντες when judgment comes; MIC 4:9-10 LXX pictures Zion-in-labor birthing her deliverance from Babylon. Second-Temple Judaism developed the chevlei Mashiach ('messianic woes') concept from this cluster. Matthew 24:8 (par. Mark 13:8) uses ἀρχὴ ὠδίνων ('beginning of birth pains') to label the preliminary tribulations.

MAT 24:15.greek_matches: LXX-composite — βδέλυγμα τῆς ἐρημώσεως is Theodotion Daniel phrasing appearing in all three Daniel loci
MAT 24:15.notes: Matthew 24:15 (par. Mark 13:14) quotes τὸ βδέλυγμα τῆς ἐρημώσεως ATTRIBUTING IT BY NAME to Δανιὴλ τοῦ προφήτου. The phrase is composite across three Daniel passages: DAN 9:27 (covenant broken + βδέλυγμα τῶν ἐρημώσεων on a wing of the temple), DAN 11:31 (Antiochus IV Epiphanes sets up τὸ βδέλυγμα ἐρημώσεως in the temple, fulfilled 167 BC with Zeus-altar), DAN 12:11 (eschatological recurrence: 1290 days after τὸ βδέλυγμα ἐρημώσεως is set up). Matthew names Daniel explicitly (Mark 13:14 has only τὸ ῥηθὲν). Matthew also adds ἐν τόπῳ ἁγίῳ (the holy place = temple) making the temple-desecration reading explicit. Historically re-applied: Caligula-statue-threat AD 40, Roman-army AD 70, future-Antichrist (2 Thess 2:3-4). One of the clearest Danielic citations in the Synoptics.

MAT 24:21.greek_matches: LXX-verbatim — ἔσται...θλῖψις οἵα οὐ γέγονεν is near-verbatim DAN 12:1 Theodotion
MAT 24:21.notes: θλῖψις μεγάλη οἵα οὐ γέγονεν ἀπ' ἀρχῆς κόσμου ἕως τοῦ νῦν echoes DAN 12:1 LXX: 'ἔσται καιρὸς θλίψεως θλῖψις οἵα οὐ γέγονεν ἀφ' οὗ γεγένηται ἔθνος ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς ἕως τοῦ καιροῦ ἐκείνου' (there shall be a time of tribulation, such tribulation as has not come since a nation was on the earth until that time). Matthew abbreviates slightly; substitutes ἀπ' ἀρχῆς κόσμου for ἀφ' οὗ γεγένηται ἔθνος — emphasizing cosmic rather than national scope. DAN 12:1 is the climactic verse of Daniel's final apocalyptic vision, introducing the resurrection (DAN 12:2). Matthew's allusion places the Olivet Discourse tribulation on the same eschatological plane as Daniel's end-time deliverance.

MAT 24:24.greek_matches: no-verbatim — σημεῖα καὶ τέρατα phrase is LXX-idiom for prophet-credentials
MAT 24:24.notes: σημεῖα μεγάλα καὶ τέρατα echoes the Deuteronomy-13 test for prophets. DEU 13:1-3 LXX: the prophet or dreamer who gives a σημεῖον ἢ τέρας that comes true but leads to worshiping other gods must be rejected and executed — the signs themselves do not validate the message if the message contradicts the Mosaic covenant. Jesus is invoking this test: even signs + wonders from false messiahs will not validate them against his own prior word. σημεῖα καὶ τέρατα is also the LXX stock pairing for the Exodus signs (DEU 4:34, 7:19, 11:3, 26:8, 29:3, 34:11) — positively framing genuine prophetic-credentials, which false prophets mimic. Matthew 24:24 and Mark 13:22 are the only NT pairing with the ψευδο- prefix (false Christs and false prophets giving signs).

MAT 24:28.notes: Jesus's proverb 'wherever the corpse is, there the vultures will gather' shares imagery with JOB 39:30 LXX ('[the vulture's] young ones dip in blood; wherever corpses may be, straightway are they found'). Relationship is diffuse — the sentiment was likely proverbial in the ancient Near East (cf. HAB 1:8 — the Chaldeans described as eagles swooping on prey). Attribution to Job is tentative. Included in DB to document the possible OT background; listed as thematic not direct since no verbal overlap with LXX.

MAT 24:29.greek_matches: LXX-composite — ὁ ἥλιος σκοτισθήσεται matches ISA 13:10 LXX; οἱ ἀστέρες πεσοῦνται matches ISA 34:4 LXX
MAT 24:29.notes: Matthew 24:29 (par. Mark 13:24-25) stitches together the OT cosmic-darkness oracle-cluster. ISA 13:10 LXX (sun darkened + stars + moon) was originally oracle-against-Babylon. ISA 34:4 LXX (stars fall, heavens roll up like scroll) was originally oracle-against-Edom. JOL 2:10, 2:31, 3:15 (sun darkened, moon to blood — cited also at ACT 2:20). EZK 32:7-8 (oracle-against-Egypt: heavens darkened, stars dimmed). AMO 8:9 (sun darkened at noon — day-of-the-LORD). In Jewish apocalyptic these cosmic-disturbance-images became standard vocabulary for the overturning of a world-order at the eschaton. 'The powers of the heavens will be shaken' may draw on ISA 34:4 LXX (τακήσονται πᾶσαι αἱ δυνάμεις τῶν οὐρανῶν) — direct LXX verbal match.

MAT 24:30.greek_matches: LXX-composite — ἐρχόμενον ἐπὶ τῶν νεφελῶν matches DAN 7:13 Theodotion (μετά → ἐπί); κόψονται πᾶσαι αἱ φυλαί matches ZEC 12:10-14 LXX
MAT 24:30.notes: Matthew 24:30 fuses two messianic-return oracles. DAN 7:13-14: 'one like a son of man coming with the clouds of heaven' receives dominion, glory, kingdom — the foundational text for Jesus' self-title 'the Son of Man' and his claim to divine authority. Matthew uses ἐπὶ τῶν νεφελῶν ('on the clouds') rather than Daniel's μετά ('with the clouds') — a shift also found in Rev 1:7. ZEC 12:10-14 predicts a day when the inhabitants of Jerusalem will mourn for 'him whom they have pierced' — Zechariah names each tribe/family 'mourning apart.' Matthew combines 'tribes of the earth mourning' (Zech 12) with 'Son of Man on clouds' (Dan 7) — the same fusion appears at REV 1:7 (which may derive from this saying of Jesus). Matthew uniquely adds 'the sign of the Son of Man will appear' — the nature of this sign has been debated for centuries (a cross? the glory-cloud itself? an astronomical sign?).

MAT 24:31.greek_matches: LXX-composite — σάλπιγγος μεγάλης matches ISA 27:13 LXX σάλπιγγι μεγάλῃ exactly
MAT 24:31.notes: Matthew 24:31 stitches together the OT eschatological-gathering oracles. ISA 27:13 LXX: "in that day they will sound the great trumpet (σαλπίσουσιν τῇ σάλπιγγι τῇ μεγάλῃ) and the lost ones in Assyria and those who perished in Egypt shall come and worship the LORD on the holy mountain in Jerusalem." Matthew adds σάλπιγγος μεγάλης (absent in Mark 13:27), directly echoing ISA 27:13. ZEC 2:6 LXX: God gathers his people ἐκ τῶν τεσσάρων ἀνέμων τοῦ οὐρανοῦ (from the four winds of heaven) — verbatim Matthew phrase. DEU 30:4 LXX: "if your dispersion is from one extremity of heaven to the other extremity, the LORD will gather you" — matches ἀπ' ἄκρων οὐρανῶν ἕως τῶν ἄκρων αὐτῶν. ZEC 9:14 LXX: the LORD blows the trumpet (σαλπιεῖ τῇ σάλπιγγι). The composite affirms: the elect will be gathered by eschatological trumpet from every direction, a cluster of OT restoration-promises now applied to the Son of Man's return.

MAT 24:35.greek_matches: no-verbatim — ὁ δὲ λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ ἡμῶν μένει εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα (ISA 40:8 LXX) is the conceptual parallel
MAT 24:35.notes: Jesus's claim 'heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away' echoes ISA 40:8 LXX ('grass withers, flower fades, but the word of our God endures forever') — applied to God's own word. Jesus applies to his own λόγοι what Isaiah reserved for the LORD's word. This is an implicit claim of divine authority, paralleling his other 'I say to you' claims in Matthew (5:22, 28, 32, etc. — contrasted with the Mosaic 'it was said'). PSA 119:89 LXX is a secondary allusion (εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα, κύριε, ὁ λόγος σου διαμένει — forever, LORD, your word abides).

MAT 25:31.greek_matches: LXX-composite — καθίσει ἐπὶ θρόνου δόξης matches Dan 7:9-10 thronework; ἔλθῃ ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἐν τῇ δόξῃ matches Dan 7:13 Son-of-Man-coming; πάντες οἱ ἄγγελοι μετ' αὐτοῦ matches Zech 14:5 LXX πάντες οἱ ἅγιοι μετ' αὐτοῦ
MAT 25:31.notes: Composite eschatological-arrival text. DAN 7:13-14 Theodotion: 'ἰδοὺ μετὰ τῶν νεφελῶν τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ὡς υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου ἐρχόμενος' — the Son-of-Man coming to receive dominion/glory/kingdom. Matthew uses ἐν τῇ δόξῃ (in his glory) for the Dan 7 glory-reception. ZEC 14:5 LXX: 'καὶ ἥξει κύριος ὁ θεός μου καὶ πάντες οἱ ἅγιοι μετ' αὐτοῦ' — the LORD coming with all his holy ones. Matthew adapts ἅγιοι → ἄγγελοι (consistent with Jewish-Second-Temple equation of 'holy ones' with angels). DAN 7:9-10 provides the throne-of-glory imagery with myriads of attendants. Second-Temple apocalyptic 1 Enoch 62:2-5 builds a similar Son-of-Man-on-throne-of-glory judgment scene. The cumulative effect: Jesus applies to himself the Danielic Son-of-Man, Zechariah's coming-LORD, and Daniel's throne-enthroned-judge — a composite Christological claim.

MAT 25:32.greek_matches: LXX-paraphrase — sheep/goats separation echoes EZK 34:17 διακρινῶ ἀνὰ μέσον προβάτου καὶ προβάτου
MAT 25:32.notes: Composite shepherd-judge + gathering-of-nations texts. EZK 34:17 LXX: 'ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ διακρινῶ ἀνὰ μέσον προβάτου καὶ προβάτου κριῶν καὶ τράγων' — YHWH-as-judge between sheep-and-sheep, rams-and-goats. The entire EZK 34 context is divine-shepherd judgment. Matthew takes the Ezek-34 shepherd-judge motif and applies it to the Son-of-Man (cf. also John 10:1-18, Rev 7:17 — Christ as the shepherd-lamb-judge). JOL 3:2 LXX (3:7 in Eng): 'συνάξω πάντα τὰ ἔθνη καὶ κατάξω αὐτοὺς εἰς τὴν κοιλάδα Ἰωσαφατ καὶ διακριθήσομαι πρὸς αὐτοὺς' — gathering-of-all-nations for judgment. ZEP 3:8 LXX: 'ἐν ἡμέρᾳ ἀναστάσεώς μου εἰς μαρτύριον... ἐκχέω ἐπ' αὐτοὺς πᾶσαν ὀργὴν θυμοῦ μου' — assembly-of-nations for judgment. Matthew 25:32 συναχθήσονται ἔμπροσθεν αὐτοῦ πάντα τὰ ἔθνη directly echoes this OT-prophetic gathering-of-nations motif.

MAT 25:34.greek_matches: no-verbatim — kingdom-inheritance motif from Daniel's visions
MAT 25:34.notes: The righteous-inheriting-the-kingdom language draws on DAN 7:18 ('the saints of the Most High will receive the kingdom and will possess the kingdom forever'), DAN 7:22 ('the saints gained possession of the kingdom'), DAN 7:27 ('the kingdom... will be given to the people of the saints of the Most High'). Matthew applies Daniel's collective 'saints of the Most High receive the kingdom' to the individual-righteous at the final judgment. The 'prepared from the foundation of the world' language is a Second-Temple election-before-creation trope (cf. 1 Enoch 48:6, 62:7); Paul develops this at EPH 1:4-5 ('chose us in him before the foundation of the world'). The blessing-of-the-Father structure inverts GEN 12:3 trajectory — Abraham's blessing-to-all-nations now bestowed by the Father through the Son-King to the righteous-of-all-nations.

MAT 25:35.greek_matches: LXX-paraphrase — Isa 58:7 ἄρτον σου τῷ πεινῶντι + στέγασον ἀνέστιον + γυμνὸν... περίβαλε provides the template for Matthew's six-item mercy-list
MAT 25:35.notes: Composite OT mercy-command cluster underlying the six-item list (hungry/thirsty/stranger/naked/sick/prison). ISA 58:6-7 LXX: 'λῦε πάντα σύνδεσμον ἀδικίας... διάθρυπτε πεινῶντι τὸν ἄρτον σου καὶ πτωχοὺς ἀστέγους εἴσαγε εἰς τὸν οἶκόν σου· ἐὰν ἴδῃς γυμνόν, περίβαλε' — break bread with the hungry, bring the homeless poor into your house, when you see naked clothe him. Isaiah's true-fast is the clearest OT template: the actions Jesus lists are precisely Isaiah's 'fast the LORD chooses.' EZK 18:7,16 LXX: the righteous-man description includes 'giving bread to the hungry and covering the naked with a garment.' JOB 31:17-22: Job's negative oath ('if I withheld bread from the poor... if I watched the fatherless without clothing...'). Matthew stitches these into a single judgment-criterion: the actions are what the OT-prophetic tradition calls covenant-faithfulness made visible. Second-Temple Jewish ethics (Tobit 4:16-17, Sirach 7:33-36) further developed this cluster as gemilut chasadim (acts-of-loving-kindness).

MAT 25:41.greek_matches: no-verbatim — πῦρ αἰώνιον shares imagery with ISA 66:24 πῦρ... οὐ σβεσθήσεται (LXX)
MAT 25:41.notes: The 'eternal fire' language draws on ISA 66:24 LXX: 'ὁ σκώληξ αὐτῶν οὐ τελευτήσει καὶ τὸ πῦρ αὐτῶν οὐ σβεσθήσεται' — unquenchable fire + undying worm as judgment-on-rebels. Jesus uses this imagery at MAT 18:8 (πῦρ αἰώνιον), MRK 9:48 (direct citation of Isa 66:24). Second-Temple apocalyptic (1 Enoch 10:13, 90:24-26; Judith 16:17) develops the eternal-fire-of-judgment motif. Significantly, Matthew 25:41 notes the fire was prepared τῷ διαβόλῳ καὶ τοῖς ἀγγέλοις αὐτοῦ (for the devil and his angels) — not for humans; humans join that destiny only by their own choice. This theological framing parallels 2 Peter 2:4 and Rev 20:10.

MAT 25:46.greek_matches: LXX-paraphrase — ζωὴν αἰώνιον matches DAN 12:2 LXX (Theodotion) ζωὴν αἰώνιον; κόλασιν αἰώνιον reshapes DAN's αἰσχύνην αἰώνιον
MAT 25:46.notes: DAN 12:2 is the clearest OT two-destinies resurrection text: 'πολλοὶ τῶν καθευδόντων ἐν γῆς χώματι ἐξεγερθήσονται, οὗτοι εἰς ζωὴν αἰώνιον καὶ οὗτοι εἰς ὀνειδισμὸν καὶ αἰσχύνην αἰώνιον' — many of those sleeping in the dust will rise, some to eternal life and some to reproach and eternal shame. Matthew preserves the ζωὴν αἰώνιον half verbatim. The punishment-half: Matthew has κόλασιν αἰώνιον where Daniel has αἰσχύνην αἰώνιον (eternal shame). The shift from shame→punishment reflects Second-Temple development of the Daniel tradition (1 Enoch 22, 103:7-8; Ps.Sol 3:11-12). The SYMMETRICAL parallel αἰώνιος-for-both is theologically weighty: whatever eternality means for life, it means for punishment. This is the strongest NT foundation for traditional eternal-conscious-punishment (against annihilationism and universalism), though the κόλασις term itself is etymologically neutral (κολάζω = discipline/punish, less harsh than τιμωρέω). Matthew 25:46 concludes the 5th and final Matthean discourse.

MAT 26:11.notes: Jesus' 'the poor you always have with you' echoes Deut 15:11 ('the poor will never cease from the land'). Not a verbatim citation — Jesus assumes the OT principle as reader-familiar background. The point is not to deprioritize almsgiving (Deut 15:11 in context commands generosity TO the poor), but to distinguish the ongoing occasion of almsgiving from the unique once-off moment of preparing Jesus for burial.

MAT 26:15.notes: The thirty pieces of silver Judas receives matches Zech 11:12 ('they weighed out for my wages thirty pieces of silver'). Matthew is alone among the Synoptics in specifying this amount — Mark 14:11 and Luke 22:5 merely say 'money.' Matthew sets up the explicit fulfillment citation in Mat 27:9-10 (compound citation attributed to Jeremiah but combining Zech 11:12-13 and Jer 32:6-15 / 19:1-13). Also echoes Exod 21:32 (price of a gored slave).

MAT 26:28.greek_matches: multi-source conceptual echoes — no verbatim quote
MAT 26:28.notes: Jesus' covenant-blood words at the Last Supper draw on multiple OT strata: (1) Exod 24:8 'blood of the covenant' — Moses sprinkling covenant blood on the people at Sinai; (2) Jer 31:31-34 — the new covenant promise (Matthew's text reads simply τῆς διαθήκης, but the soteriological framing 'for forgiveness of sins' echoes Jer 31:34 'I will remember their sin no more'); (3) Isa 53:11-12 — the Suffering Servant 'poured out his soul to death... he bore the sin of many' (περὶ πολλῶν matches LXX); (4) Zec 9:11 'blood of my covenant' for prisoner-release. TEXTUAL NOTE: SBLGNT reads 'the covenant' (shorter); many MSS add 'new' (harmonization with Luke 22:20 / 1 Cor 11:25). Modern evangelical Bibles all follow SBLGNT here. Matthew's unique εἰς ἄφεσιν ἁμαρτιῶν ('for forgiveness of sins') makes the atonement function explicit.

MAT 26:29.notes: Jesus' vow to drink 'anew' in his Father's kingdom echoes Isa 25:6, the eschatological banquet on the mountain with aged wine. The messianic-banquet motif is a widely-attested Second Temple Jewish hope. Matthean distinctive: 'in my Father's kingdom' (vs. Mark 14:25's 'kingdom of God').

MAT 26:31.greek_matches: adapted form — first person 'I will strike' vs. MT imperative 'strike!' and LXX plural imperative πατάξατε
MAT 26:31.notes: Explicit OT citation (γέγραπται formula). Jesus applies Zech 13:7 to himself as the stricken shepherd. Matthew/Mark agree on the adapted first-person form πατάξω ('I [God] will strike') — diverging from both the MT imperative (הַךְ, hak) and LXX plural imperative (πατάξατε). The adaptation likely reflects a Jewish targum-style interpretive tradition where God becomes the implicit striker. Fulfilled at v.56 when the disciples flee.

MAT 26:52.notes: Jesus' proverbial saying 'all who take the sword will perish by the sword' possibly echoes Gen 9:6 ('whoever sheds human blood, by humans shall their blood be shed'). A general lex-talionis framework rather than a direct citation. Unique to Matthew among the Synoptics (no Markan or Lukan parallel for this saying).

MAT 26:64.greek_matches: composite allusion — Ps 110:1 enthronement + Dan 7:13 coming-on-clouds
MAT 26:64.notes: Double OT allusion. 'Sitting at the right hand of Power' = Ps 110:1 LXX ('sit at my right hand'), with 'Power' (ἡ δύναμις) as a reverent Jewish circumlocution for the divine name. 'Coming on the clouds of heaven' = Dan 7:13 LXX (ὡς υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου ἐπὶ τῶν νεφελῶν τοῦ οὐρανοῦ). Jesus' statement combines the enthroned Davidic king (Ps 110) with the cosmic Son of Man (Dan 7) — the most explicit Christological self-identification in the Synoptics. Matthean distinctive ἀπ' ἄρτι ('from now on') absent from Mark 14:62.

MAT 27:4.notes: Judas's αἷμα ἀθῷον ('innocent blood') confession echoes Deut 27:25 LXX (ἐπικατάρατος ὃς ἂν λάβῃ δῶρα πατάξαι ψυχὴν αἵματος ἀθῴου — 'cursed is anyone who takes a bribe to slay an innocent soul of blood'). Judas takes the bribe and slays the innocent — the Deuteronomic curse falls on him. Also resonates with Jer 26:15 (Jeremiah's warning to Jerusalem leadership about innocent blood). The OT-language of innocent-blood-shed-on-the-land carries covenant-curse weight.

MAT 27:6.notes: The chief priests' refusal to put 'blood-money' in the κορβανᾶς (temple treasury) reflects Deut 23:18 LXX (forbidden funds — abominations not received at the LORD's house) extended-by-analogy + Deut 27:25 (curse on those who take bribes for innocent blood). Matthean irony: the priests are scrupulous about Deuteronomic-purity-of-funds while having engineered the Deuteronomic-cursed deed (innocent-blood-shedding for bribery — exactly Deut 27:25). The legal-scrupulosity covering moral-corruption follows the prophetic critique pattern (Isa 1:11-17).

MAT 27:7.notes: The 'potter's field' purchase establishes the typological field for the Jer/Zech composite citation that explodes in v.9. Jer 18 (potter and clay) + Jer 19 (potter's earthen vessel broken at Topheth as judgment, with explicit innocent-blood imagery — Jer 19:4) + Zech 11:13 (30 silver thrown to the potter in the LORD's house) all converge on this purchase. Matthew narrates the action in v.7 and cites the prophetic-fulfillment in vv.9-10. The buying of the field IS the typological event.

MAT 27:9.notes: MAJOR COMPOSITE CITATION. Matthew attributes to Jeremiah but the verbal-shape is closest to Zech 11:12-13 LXX (the 30 pieces of silver thrown 'to the potter in the house of the LORD'). Jeremiah-thread is real and significant: Jer 19:1-13 (potter's earthen vessel broken at Topheth as judgment, with explicit 'innocent blood' imagery — Jer 19:4 αἷμα ἀθῷον, exactly Matthew's phrase from v.4) and Jer 32:6-15 (Jeremiah buys a field, redemption-typology). Matthew's attribution to the major prophet (Jeremiah) follows standard Jewish composite-citation convention (cf. Mark 1:2-3 attributed to Isaiah though it includes Mal 3:1). The fulfillment is editorial/typological, not predictive in a flat sense — the Jeremiah-Zechariah composite reads the priests' purchase as the convergence of (a) judgment on Jerusalem leadership for shedding innocent blood [Jer 19] and (b) the despised valuation of God's true shepherd [Zech 11]. Per RULES.md §5: documented as composite citation with Jer-major, Zech-verbal.

MAT 27:10.notes: Continuation of the Jer/Zech composite citation begun in v.9. 'They gave them for the potter's field, as the Lord commanded me' adapts Zech 11:13 LXX (καὶ ἐνέβαλον αὐτοὺς εἰς τὸν οἶκον κυρίου εἰς τὸ χωνευτήριον — 'I cast them into the house of the LORD, into the foundry'). Matthew's καθὰ συνέταξέν μοι κύριος = 'as the Lord commanded me' is the prophet's first-person voice. The 'me' in the citation refers to Zechariah/Jeremiah; Matthew applies it to the whole prophetic-witness-tradition.

MAT 27:12.notes: Jesus's silence before accusers (Mat 27:12, 14) deliberately echoes Isa 53:7 LXX (αὐτὸς δὲ διὰ τὸ κεκακῶσθαι οὐκ ἀνοίγει τὸ στόμα — 'though afflicted, he does not open his mouth, like a sheep led to slaughter'). Pilate's amazement (v.14) makes most sense in light of this prophesied silence. Same suffering-servant motif also at Mark 14:60-61 (silence before Caiaphas). One of the strongest non-explicit Isa-53 fulfillments in the passion narrative.

MAT 27:30.notes: ἐμπτύσαντες εἰς αὐτὸν ('they spat on him') echoes Isa 50:6 LXX (τὸ πρόσωπόν μου οὐκ ἀπέστρεψα ἀπὸ αἰσχύνης ἐμπτυσμάτων — 'I did not turn my face from the shame of spittings'). Same root ἐμπτύω. The Servant-of-Yahweh's voluntary acceptance of mockery and spitting. Matthew preserves the verbal-thread without explicit citation.

MAT 27:34.notes: Matthew's χολῆς ('gall') in the wine-mixed-with-gall is a deliberate Greek-OT-citation alignment with LXX Ps 69:22 (καὶ ἔδωκαν εἰς τὸ βρῶμά μου χολήν — 'they gave gall for my food'). Mark 15:23 has σμυρνισμένον ('myrrhed wine,' analgesic), no χολή. Matthew's editorial change to χολή is intentional — preserving the Ps 69 framework (which returns at v.48 with vinegar). Ps 69 + Ps 22 are Matthew's twin controlling intertexts for the crucifixion-details.

MAT 27:35.notes: Matthew's διεμερίσαντο τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ βάλλοντες κλῆρον is a near-verbatim alignment with LXX Ps 22:19 (διεμερίσαντο τὰ ἱμάτιά μου ἑαυτοῖς, καὶ ἐπὶ τὸν ἱματισμόν μου ἔβαλον κλῆρον). John 19:24 makes the citation explicit; Matthew records the fulfillment without adding a citation formula. TEXTUAL VARIANT: some later MSS expand v.35 with the explicit fulfillment-formula; SBLGNT ends at κλῆρον. We follow SBLGNT (shorter reading; the longer is a harmonization to John 19:24).

MAT 27:38.notes: Two-robbers crucified-with-Jesus echoes Isa 53:12 LXX (καὶ ἐν τοῖς ἀνόμοις ἐλογίσθη — 'he was numbered with the lawless'). Mark 15:28 makes this citation explicit (though Mark 15:28 is a textual variant; some MSS omit). Luke 22:37 has Jesus self-cite this verse before the betrayal. Matthew records the fulfillment without adding the citation-formula. The two-criminals-flanking detail is part of the Servant-suffering pattern.

MAT 27:39.notes: Matthew's κινοῦντες τὰς κεφαλὰς αὐτῶν ('shaking their heads') is verbatim from LXX Ps 22:8 (πάντες οἱ θεωροῦντές με ἐξεμυκτήρισάν με, ἐλάλησαν ἐν χείλεσιν, ἐκίνησαν κεφαλήν). Confirms Ps 22 as Matthew's controlling intertext for the crucifixion. Head-wagging is a stock OT taunt-gesture (also Ps 109:25; Job 16:4; Lam 2:15) but the Matthean wording specifically matches Ps 22:7 LXX.

MAT 27:43.notes: The leaders' taunt 'He trusts in God; let God rescue him now if he wants him' is verbal-fulfillment of Ps 22:8 LXX (ἤλπισεν ἐπὶ κύριον, ῥυσάσθω αὐτόν· σωσάτω αὐτόν, ὅτι θέλει αὐτόν — 'he hoped on the Lord, let him deliver him; let him save him, because he wants him'). Matthew's πέποιθεν (perfect of πείθω) adapts the LXX ἤλπισεν (aorist of ἐλπίζω) — synonymic-shift but the verbal-shape is unmistakable. The leaders unwittingly speak the prophesied taunt — completing Matthew's Ps 22 framework. Their rejection of Jesus's 'Son of God' claim ironically affirms the title's centrality.

MAT 27:45.notes: Three-hour supernatural darkness at noon echoes Amos 8:9 LXX (καὶ δύσεται ὁ ἥλιος μεσημβρίας, καὶ συσκοτάσει ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς ἐν ἡμέρᾳ τὸ φῶς — 'the sun shall set at noon, and the light shall be darkened on the earth by day'). Day-of-the-LORD imagery. Also resonates with Joel 2:31, 3:15 (cosmic darkness as judgment-sign) and Exod 10:21-23 (plague-darkness over Egypt). The 3 hours of darkness at full moon (Passover) cannot be solar eclipse — it is supernatural sign, framed as cosmic mourning + divine judgment.

MAT 27:46.notes: MOST EXPLICIT Ps 22 citation — Jesus quotes the opening line in his native Aramaic (Ἠλὶ ἠλὶ λεμὰ σαβαχθάνι), then Matthew translates. Matthew's Greek (Θεέ μου θεέ μου, ἱνατί με ἐγκατέλιπες) closely matches LXX Ps 22:2 (ὁ θεὸς ὁ θεός μου, πρόσχες μοι· ἵνα τί ἐγκατέλιπές με). Ancient Jewish convention: citing the first line of a psalm invokes the WHOLE psalm — including its triumphant ending (Ps 22:22-31, deliverance and worldwide-praise). Jesus's cry expresses both real abandonment-experience AND invocation of the psalm's promised vindication. Matthew's Ἠλί (Hebrew form, vs. Mark's Aramaic Ἐλωῒ) explains the Elijah-misidentification in v.47.

MAT 27:48.notes: Matthew's ὄξος ('sour wine/vinegar') is verbatim from LXX Ps 69:22 (καὶ εἰς τὴν δίψαν μου ἐπότισάν με ὄξος — 'and for my thirst they gave me vinegar to drink'). Pairs with v.34's χολή — together Matthew constructs the Ps 69 fulfillment-frame around the Ps 22 frame. John 19:28-29 makes the Ps 69 citation explicit; Matthew records the fulfillment without explicit citation-formula. The relief-vs-mockery ambiguity of the offering preserves Matthew's narrative flexibility.

MAT 27:57.notes: Joseph of Arimathea as ἄνθρωπος πλούσιος ('rich man') echoes Isa 53:9 LXX (καὶ δώσω τοὺς πονηροὺς ἀντὶ τῆς ταφῆς αὐτοῦ καὶ τοὺς πλουσίους ἀντὶ τοῦ θανάτου αὐτοῦ — 'I will give the wicked in exchange for his burial and the rich in exchange for his death'). Matthew's emphasis on Joseph as πλούσιος — absent from Mark 15:43 — is deliberate Isa-53 alignment. Jesus is 'with the rich' in his death precisely as Isaiah prophesied. Matthew uniquely highlights this typological detail.

MAT 27:60.notes: Joseph's new rock-cut tomb continues the Isa 53:9 fulfillment-thread begun in v.57 (πλούσιος). Isa 53:9 LXX (καὶ δώσω τοὺς πονηροὺς ἀντὶ τῆς ταφῆς αὐτοῦ καὶ τοὺς πλουσίους ἀντὶ τοῦ θανάτου αὐτοῦ — 'I will give the wicked in exchange for his burial and the rich in exchange for his death'). The wicked are given (ἀντὶ τῆς ταφῆς) — Jesus is buried alongside criminals' execution-site, but in a rich-man's tomb. The dual fulfillment is precise: cross with criminals + tomb of the rich. Matthew is the most explicit Synoptist on this Isa-53 alignment.

MAT 28:3.notes: The angel's appearance fuses two Danielic theophany images: (a) Dan 7:9 LXX — 'his clothing was white as snow' (τὸ ἔνδυμα αὐτοῦ ὡσεὶ χιὼν λευκόν) — Matthew's τὸ ἔνδυμα αὐτοῦ λευκὸν ὡς χιών is a near-verbatim echo; (b) Dan 10:6 — Daniel's vision of a heavenly figure whose 'face was like the appearance of lightning.' Matthew paints the tomb-angel in the color-palette of Daniel's most exalted theophanies, marking this messenger as from the same cosmic-sovereignty register as the resurrection he announces. Same color-imagery recurs at the Transfiguration (17:2).

MAT 28:9.notes: The women's prostration and foot-grasping fulfill the Royal Psalm pattern — Ps 2:11-12 LXX ('serve the Lord with fear and rejoice in him with trembling; embrace correction [δράξασθε παιδείας]') and Ps 72:11 ('all kings shall bow down to him, all nations shall serve him'). The first post-resurrection act is worship (προσκύνησις) — the same act given by the Magi at Jesus's birth (MAT 2:2, 2:11), forming a Matthean inclusio of full-worship from infancy to resurrection.

MAT 28:16.notes: The Galilean mountain as locus for the Great Commission echoes two OT mountain-revelation texts: (a) Exod 19:20 — Sinai as the mountain where covenant is given — Matthew's Jesus as new-Moses issuing a new covenant-commission; (b) Isa 2:2-3 — 'the mountain of the house of the LORD shall be established...and all nations shall flow to it...out of Zion shall go forth the law' — prophetic picture of the nations drawn to God's mountain. Matthew inverts the Isa 2 direction: rather than nations flowing TO the mountain, the commissioned disciples go OUT from the mountain to all nations. The Matthean mountain-motif (4:8, 5:1, 14:23, 15:29, 17:1, 24:3, 28:16) climaxes here.

MAT 28:18.notes: CENTRAL MATTHEAN CHRISTOLOGICAL CLAIM. Dan 7:13-14 LXX: 'And behold, with the clouds of heaven came one like a Son of Man...and to him was given dominion (ἐξουσία) and glory and a kingdom, that all peoples, nations, and languages should serve him.' Jesus's Ἐδόθη μοι πᾶσα ἐξουσία ἐν οὐρανῷ καὶ ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς is the Son-of-Man enthronement of Dan 7 declared in the first person. Matthew has been building this identification throughout the gospel through the 'Son of Man' title (16:27-28, 24:30, 25:31, 26:64); now the risen Christ self-claims Daniel's vision. The divine passive ἐδόθη preserves the Father-gives-Son structure seen also in Phil 2:9-11 ('God highly exalted him'). This verse is the logical and theological foundation for the Commission that follows in vv.19-20: because Jesus has ALL authority (πᾶσα ἐξουσία = Dan 7 universal dominion), therefore the disciples are sent to ALL nations (πάντα τὰ ἔθνη = Dan 7 πάντα τὰ ἔθνη serving the Son).

MAT 28:19.notes: πάντα τὰ ἔθνη ('all the nations') consciously fulfills the universal-scope of the Abrahamic covenant and prophetic hope: (a) Gen 12:3 — 'in you shall all the families of the earth be blessed' — foundational Abrahamic promise now actualized through Jesus's disciples; (b) Isa 2:2-3 — all nations streaming to the mountain of the Lord — reversed as the commissioned go out; (c) Ps 2:8 — 'ask of me and I will make the nations your heritage' — the Son's claim on the nations granted at enthronement; (d) Dan 7:14 — 'all peoples, nations, and languages should serve him' — extending v.18's Dan 7 frame. The Matthean restriction-then-universalization arc (10:5-6 'go not to the Gentiles' → 15:24 'only to the lost sheep of Israel' → 28:19 'all nations') traces the redemptive-historical unfolding from covenant-Israel-first to universal-scope post-resurrection.

MAT 28:20.notes: MATTHEAN INCLUSIO with 1:23 (ἐμμανουήλ = μεθ᾽ ἡμῶν ὁ θεός). Jesus's closing ἐγὼ μεθ᾽ ὑμῶν εἰμι is a first-person appropriation of the covenantal divine-presence formula: 'I am/will-be with you' — the hallmark assurance spoken by God to Isaac (Gen 26:3, 24), Jacob (Gen 28:15, 31:3), Moses (Exod 3:12), Joshua (Josh 1:5, 9), Gideon (Judg 6:16), Jeremiah (Jer 1:8, 19), and eschatologically Isa 41:10, 43:5, Hag 1:13. Jesus speaks this divine-prerogative-formula in his own voice, applying it to the church. The 'to the end of the age' extension pushes this presence-promise to the eschatological horizon — Christ's continuing presence (not absence) is the frame within which the mission of vv.19-20 unfolds. No ascension is narrated in Matthew — the risen Jesus remains present, abiding with his disciples until the συντέλεια.

MRK 1:2.notes: Mark attributes to Isaiah but quotes Malachi+Exodus. The 'ἄγγελόν μου' phrase matches LXX Mal 3:1 closely.

MRK 1:3.notes: Voice-in-wilderness text matches LXX ordering ('in the wilderness') rather than MT ('call in the wilderness prepare the way').

MRK 1:11.notes: Baptism voice combines Royal-Son (Ps 2), Servant-beloved (Isa 42), and beloved-sacrificial-son (Gen 22).

MRK 2:26.notes: David eating the Bread of the Presence (1 Sam 21:1-6). HISTORICAL CRUX: Mark's ἐπὶ Ἀβιαθάρ ἀρχιερέως — 1 Sam 21 records Ahimelech, not Abiathar, as priest at Nob; Abiathar (his son) succeeded him (1 Sam 22:20-23). Solutions in literature: (1) Mark/Jesus conflates by memory; (2) ἐπί + gen. = 'in the Scripture section named for Abiathar' (paralleling Mark 12:26's ἐπὶ τοῦ βάτου 'in the burning-bush passage'); (3) both shared priesthood during transition; (4) a variant reading. SBLGNT retains Ἀβιαθάρ as the harder reading. Jesus uses the David-precedent to defend disciples' sabbath plucking — human need supersedes ritual boundary.

MRK 3:13.notes: Jesus ascending the mountain to call the Twelve echoes Moses ascending Sinai to establish the covenant people (Exod 24:1-11). Mark's 'he went up the mountain and called whom he wanted' carries Moses-typology — the new people-of-God being constituted on a mountain by the Son.

MRK 4:3.notes: Jesus's opening 'Listen!' (Ἀκούετε) echoes the Shema's opening imperative (Deut 6:4 'Hear, O Israel'). The Parable of the Sower is structured as Jesus's own Shema — a call to covenant-listening that becomes the criterion for understanding all his parables (v.13).

MRK 4:12.notes: Mark's quotation diverges from LXX Isa 6:9–10 in a crucial way: LXX ends with ἰάσομαι αὐτούς ('I will heal them'); Mark has ἀφεθῇ αὐτοῖς ('it might be forgiven them'). This aligns with Targum Jonathan on Isa 6:10, suggesting Mark draws on an Aramaic paraphrase tradition rather than pure LXX. The substitution 'forgive' for 'heal' theologically reads physical restoration as spiritual forgiveness. Famous 'parables harden' crux (matt 13:13, Luke 8:10 soften; Mark preserves the sharpness).

MRK 4:29.notes: Mark 4:29 'εὐθὺς ἀποστέλλει τὸ δρέπανον, ὅτι παρέστηκεν ὁ θερισμός' directly echoes Joel 3:13 LXX (4:13 LXX numbering: ἐξαποστείλατε δρέπανα, ὅτι παρέστηκεν τρυγητός). Isa 17:5 provides secondary resonance. The harvest-sickle is standard OT imagery for eschatological divine judgment (cf. Rev 14:14-16). The parable's force: between sowing and reaping is hidden divine growth, which crashes into visibility at harvest.

MRK 4:32.notes: Mustard seed's birds-in-shade imagery alludes to OT cosmic-tree texts where birds-of-heaven dwelling in a great tree's branches = nations finding refuge in an empire (Ezek 17:23 Davidic cedar; Ezek 31:6 Assyrian cedar; Dan 4:12 Nebuchadnezzar's tree). Jesus subversively downgrades the cosmic cedar to a humble garden bush — but the refuge-for-nations sense is preserved.

MRK 4:41.notes: The disciples' 'Who is this?' invokes OT sea-subduing theophany: Ps 89:8-9 ('O LORD God of hosts, who is like you? You still the raging of the sea'); Ps 107:29 ('he stilled the storm to a whisper'); Job 38:8-11 (YHWH sets bounds to the sea). For first-century Jews, only YHWH commands the sea. Mark places this Christological question at the climax of ch.4 to force the same confession the centurion will voice at 15:39 ('Truly this was the Son of God'). THEME CLOSURE: ch.4 opens with 'Listen!' (v.3) and closes with 'the wind and sea OBEY' (ὑπακούει = 'hear-under') — creation hears what the disciples struggle to.

MRK 6:23.notes: Herod's 'up to half my kingdom' oath echoes Est 5:3, 5:6, 7:2 (Ahasuerus to Esther). Mark's ironic reversal: in Esther the formula saves Jewish lives; here it costs a prophet's. The escalation v.22 casual offer → v.23 sworn oath traps Herod in front of his court.

MRK 6:34.notes: Jesus's compassion at 'sheep without a shepherd' (ὡς πρόβατα μὴ ἔχοντα ποιμένα) tracks Num 27:17 LXX (ὡσεὶ πρόβατα οἷς οὐκ ἔστιν ποιμήν). Secondary allusions: 1 Kgs 22:17; Ezek 34:5; Zech 10:2. Num 27 is Joshua's commissioning as new-shepherd of Israel — Jesus as new-Joshua leading the wilderness-people-who-need-a-shepherd. σπλαγχνίζομαι (compassion) is Mark's signature verb for Jesus's shepherd-instinct. The chapter opened with rejection in his hometown; it ends with overflowing compassion for strangers.

MRK 6:48.notes: Walking-on-water + 'wished to pass them by' is layered OT theophany-language. (1) Job 9:8 LXX — only YHWH treads on the sea. (2) Exod 33:19, 22 — God 'passes by' Moses on the rock; Mark's παρελθεῖν echoes LXX παρελεύσομαι. (3) 1 Kgs 19:11 LXX — God 'passes by' (παρελεύσεται) Elijah at Horeb. Jesus's walking-on-water is a deliberate theophanic enactment: doing what YHWH does in OT theophanies, using OT theophany-vocabulary. The disciples nearly recognize the supernatural quality but misinterpret it as apparition (v.49). Fourth-watch timing (3-6 AM) after ~9 hours of rowing against the wind places the miracle at maximum exhaustion.

MRK 6:50.notes: ἐγώ εἰμι: Mark's most theologically charged self-disclosure. Echoes Exod 3:14 LXX (God's name to Moses) and Isa 41:4 / 43:10 / 43:25 / 51:12 / 52:6 (YHWH's self-identification 'I am he' / 'I am'). Combined with v.48's theophanic 'pass by' and walking-on-water (Job 9:8), Mark constructs a sustained YHWH-self-revelation tableau. The disciples should hear: this is GOD revealing himself in the storm. Their non-recognition (v.52 hearts hardened) is the tragedy. CHRISTOLOGICAL CRUX: Mark 6:50 is load-bearing for the high Christology — Jesus does what only YHWH does, says what only YHWH says. Thai 'เราเอง' dual-functions as natural self-identifier AND emphatic 'I AM.'

MRK 6:52.notes: πωρόω/πώρωσις (heart-hardness) carries Exodus-vocabulary — originally Pharaoh-language (Exod 7:3; 8:15; 9:12). Mark applies it to the Twelve, their Pharaoh-level resistance to repeated revelation. Paul picks up the same word-family for unbelieving Israel (Rom 11:25). Mark's theological point: the gospel doesn't assume the disciples' unique virtue — they too need the hardening broken open by repeated revelation.

MRK 7:6.notes: Jesus quotes Isa 29:13 LXX closely. The MT differs ('their fear of me is a commandment of men learned by rote'); the LXX phrasing 'in vain do they worship me, teaching as doctrines the commandments of men' is what Jesus' argument depends on. One of the clearer cases in Mark where the citation specifically tracks LXX rather than MT — confirms Mark's audience reads Greek scripture. Full citation runs vv.6b–7.

MRK 7:7.notes: Continuation of Isa 29:13 LXX citation begun in v.6. Note: Mark's word order (διδασκαλίας ἐντάλματα ἀνθρώπων) differs slightly from standard LXX (ἐντάλματα ἀνθρώπων καὶ διδασκαλίας) — either a textual variant of LXX known to Mark or Mark's free citation. Theological force unchanged.

MRK 7:10.notes: Two OT citations paired: (1) the 5th commandment to honor parents (Exod 20:12 / Deut 5:16); (2) the death penalty for parental cursing (Exod 21:17 / Lev 20:9). Both quotations align with LXX. Jesus pairs them deliberately to show that the Mosaic obligation toward parents is non-negotiable — a positive command and a negative sanction together establishing unchallengeable weight. The Corban-loophole in vv.11–12 violates BOTH the command and undermines the sanction.

MRK 7:32.notes: The rare word μογιλάλος ('hardly able to speak') used to describe the brought-to-Jesus man echoes LXX Isa 35:6 directly: 'τρανὴ ἔσται γλῶσσα μογιλάλων' ('the tongue of the dumb shall sing for joy'). Combined with κωφός (deaf, Isa 35:5 LXX 'the ears of the deaf'), Mark deliberately marks this healing as fulfillment of Isa 35's Messianic-restoration promises. μογιλάλος is rare in all of Greek literature — Mark's choice is not casual. Sets up v.37.

MRK 7:35.notes: Isa 35:5-6 Messianic-age healing-promise: 'the eyes of the blind shall be opened, and the ears of the deaf shall be unstopped... the tongue of the dumb (μογιλάλων) shall sing for joy.' Mark's vocabulary tracks Isaiah's promised restoration precisely (μογιλάλος is a LXX hapax shared with Isa 35:6). The instantaneous reversal — ears opened, tongue loosed, plain speech — enacts the Messianic age. Double passive (ἠνοίγησαν, ἐλύθη) is divine-passive: agency is Jesus's-command-as-God's-power.

MRK 7:37.notes: Crowd's confession 'πάντα καλῶς πεποίηκεν' (he has done all things well) echoes Gen 1:31 LXX 'εἶδεν ὁ θεὸς τὰ πάντα ὅσα ἐποίησεν, καὶ ἰδοὺ καλὰ λίαν' — attributing creational-good language to Jesus' deeds. The specifics 'makes the deaf hear and the mute speak' echo Isa 35:5–6 LXX Messianic-restoration signs (ἀκούσονται κωφοί, γλῶσσα μογιλάλων / ἀλάλων). Together: Mark frames Jesus' Gentile-territory ministry as both new-creational (Gen 1) and Messianic-restorative (Isa 35). The Gentile crowd is unwittingly orthodox — confessing what the religious establishment of vv.1–13 refused to see.

MRK 8:18.notes: Echo of Jer 5:21 LXX (ὀφθαλμοὶ αὐτοῖς καὶ οὐ βλέπουσιν, ὦτα αὐτοῖς καὶ οὐκ ἀκούουσιν) and Ezek 12:2 LXX (similar phrasing). Both prophetic indictments of apostate Israel; Mark reapplies them to the Twelve. Also resonates with Isa 6:9–10 (already cited at Mark 4:12). The intertextual jolt is theologically devastating: the disciples function as Jesus' 'outsiders.' Inner-quotes used in Thai output to mark the OT echo.

MRK 8:31.notes: μετὰ τρεῖς ἡμέρας ἀναστῆναι ('after three days rise') possible echo of Hos 6:2 LXX μετὰ δύο ἡμέρας ... ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ ἀναστησόμεθα ('after two days...on the third day we shall rise'). Mark's 'after three days' is the harder reading; Matt/Luke harmonize to 'on the third day' (more directly matching Hos 6:2's day-three). ἀποδοκιμασθῆναι in same verse echoes Ps 118:22 LXX (cited later at Mark 12:10).

MRK 8:37.notes: Echo of Ps 49:7–8 (LXX 48:8–9): 'no man can ransom his brother, or give to God a price for his life ... for the ransom of their soul is costly.' Wisdom-tradition allusion shaping Jesus' rhetorical impossibility. Not direct citation.

MRK 8:38.notes: Son-of-Man-coming-in-glory-with-holy-ones combines Dan 7:13–14 LXX (eschatological Son of Man receiving dominion) with Zech 14:5 LXX (YHWH 'comes with all the holy ones'). The 'holy ones' tradition shifts from heavenly-host (Zech) to angels (Mark) — both Jewish-apocalyptic categories blending. First explicit 'Father' (πατήρ) reference Jesus makes of himself in Mark — opens the Son-Father christological depth that unfolds in Mark 9 (Transfiguration) and 14:36 (Gethsemane Abba).

MRK 9:2.notes: Six-day delay echoes Exod 24:15-16 (Moses on Sinai: six days of waiting, then YHWH's voice on the seventh). Jesus takes the inner-three — Peter, James, John — the same three who will sleep in Gethsemane (14:33). The Transfiguration narratively confirms 8:38 ('the Son of Man will come in the glory of his Father'): here that glory is momentarily unveiled. The Moses-typology runs throughout (mountain, cloud, voice, glory).

MRK 9:3.notes: Two hapax in a single verse (στίλβω 'shine-radiantly', γναφεύς 'launderer') render the heavenly-whiteness superlatively. Parallels: Dan 7:9 LXX (Ancient of Days: τὸ ἔνδυμα αὐτοῦ ὡσεὶ χιὼν λευκόν 'his clothing white as snow') and Dan 10:6 (man-clothed-in-linen with body στίλβοντος as beryl). Mark's diction evokes apocalyptic theophany — Jesus visually identified with the Dan 7 divine-figure.

MRK 9:7.notes: The divine voice combines: Deut 18:15 LXX (αὐτοῦ ἀκούσεσθε 'you shall listen to him' — the prophet-like-Moses); Ps 2:7 (Davidic-royal 'you are my son'); Isa 42:1 (servant-beloved ὁ ἐκλεκτός μου). Command 'hear him' (ἀκούετε αὐτοῦ) is the transfiguration climax: Jesus supersedes Law-and-Prophets (embodied by Moses and Elijah present) as THE one to be heard. The cloud (Shekhinah) evokes Exod 24 + Exod 40:34 + Num 7:89 theophany-sequence. Not 'one of three' (as Peter proposed in v.5) but the unique Son.

MRK 9:11.notes: Disciples' question invokes the scribal doctrine derived from Mal 4:5-6 (Mal 3:23-24 Heb.): Elijah must come before the Messiah. Having just seen Elijah on the mountain (v.4), they ask whether the prophecy is fulfilled in what they just witnessed, or whether there is a future-Elijah. Jesus's reply (vv.12-13) identifies John the Baptist as the fulfillment (cf. Mark 1:6 — John's dress explicitly echoes Elijah's 2 Kgs 1:8 LXX).

MRK 9:12.notes: Jesus's question about the Son of Man being 'treated with contempt' (ἐξουδενηθῇ, hapax in Mark) echoes the Suffering-Servant rejection language of Isa 53:3 LXX and Ps 22:6 LXX (related form ἐξουθενέω).
